---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

class: slideblue

.size80[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size45[
The Role of Stakeholders in Policy-making
- Case Study: US CAFE Standards
]

<br>

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2022)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. Make sure to save time at end of class to discuss Thursday plan and the paper projects.

2. Update slide on process developed in class last time

3. Post assignment description on Moodle

.size10[
Readings

1. Miller, N. (Ed.). (2009). Corporate Average Fuel Economy Regulations (CAFE Standards). In *Environmental Politics: Stakeholders, Interests, and Policymaking*. (pp. 106–141). Routledge.

2. Grandoni, D., Siddiqui , F. & Phillips, A. (2021, Dec 20). New Biden rule reducing climate emissions from cars and SUVs reverses major Trump rollback. *The Washington Post*. [Link](https://www.washingtonpost.com/climate-environment/2021/12/20/auto-mileage-rule-biden-climate/)
]




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01-1-ozarks_forest.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center, inverse

.size55[**The "Politics" of the "Wilderness"**]

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

.size55[**"Environmental" "Policy"**]

???

### Ok, policy designers, what are the key lessons to keep in mind from our first two weeks of work?

#### - What has stood out for you?

*Discuss*

<br>

SLIDE: Let's highlight some key elements that I believe are important for us as real-world problem-solvers.





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01-1-ozarks_forest.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center, inverse

.size55[**The "Politics" of the "Wilderness"**]

<br>

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='52%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/03-1-nos_yeses.jpg')
```

<br>

.size55[**"Environmental" "Policy"**]

???

### First, to be an effective policy designer you MUST have a model of politics in mind to guide your planning

<br>

We explored and developed a definition of politics meant to help us think about how societies make and enforce rules of behavior

+ Actors pursuing their goals, constrained by institutional rules and colliding with each other while they do it. 

<br>

Being strategic means being thoughtful about the dynamics of the situtation you are facing.

<br>

My hope is that thinking about your environmental problem in terms of interests, institutions and interactions will help you develop a strategy.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01-1-ozarks_forest.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center, inverse

.size55[**The "Politics" of the "Wilderness"**]

<br>

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='52%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/03-1-flower_pavement.jpg')
```

<br>

.size55[**"Environmental" "Policy"**]

???

### Second, to be an effective policy designer you MUST be prepared for different stakeholders to define the key concepts differently

<br>

We explored the "environment" as a contested concept using the Cronon reading in order to help us:

1. To expect different definitions of any underlying problem, 

2. To think critically about the arguments that drive you, especially the ones you haven't clarified for yourself, and 

3. To make sure our proposals, and our expectations for human behavior, are NOT based on myths.


 

---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01-1-ozarks_forest.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center, inverse

.size55[**The "Politics" of the "Wilderness"**]

<br>

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='53%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/03-1-new_plan.jpg')
```

<br>

.size55[**"Environmental" "Policy"**]

???

### Third, to be an effective policy designer your policy SHOULD include three elements:

1. A definite course or method of action,

2. An evaluation showing your plan is "better" than a set of realistic alternatives, and

3. Evidence that your plan is adapted to the "given conditions" of the problem.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01-1-ozarks_forest.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center, inverse

.size55[**The "Politics" of the "Wilderness"**]

<br>

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='55%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/02-1-cavemen.jpg')
```

<br>

.size55[**"Environmental" "Policy"**]

???

### Fourth, to be an effective policy designer you MUST think carefully about why societies create policies in the first place.

Keep in mind as you work:

First, all societies face resource issues and resource issues create incentives to free-ride
- Your policy has to work in a world where some members of the community can benefit without contributing
    
<br>

Second, policy design fights are often fights about the right source of "wisdom" or "accountability"
- "Leave it to the markets" or "let's have a referendum" or "trust the science" are all arguments about problem framing that simply distribute the costs and benefits in society differently.

- YOU should focus on the problem and learn to see these strategies as somewhat interchangeable based on the circumstances




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01-1-ozarks_forest.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center, inverse

.size55[**The "Politics" of the "Wilderness"**]

<br>

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='55%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/02-1-cavemen.jpg')
```

<br>

.size55[**"Environmental" "Policy"**]

???

Third, policy can only ever be as effective as the authority that compels the behavior
- Finding the "right" kind of "authority" can help alleviate the free-rider problem. 
    
<br>

Fourth, every policy proposal must be built on three arguments BEFORE getting into the details of the actual policy! 

1. You MUST make an argument that WE have a problem, 

2. You MUST make an argument that WE should decide collectively, not privately, and 

3. You MUST make an argument about how the rule shoudl be decided (democracy? delegation to experts?)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01-1-ozarks_forest.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center, inverse

.size55[**The "Politics" of the "Wilderness"**]

<br>

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='62%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/03-1-problem_solving_process.gif')
```

<br>

.size55[**"Environmental" "Policy"**]

???

### Finally, to be an effective policy designer you SHOULD consider how established processes of environmental conflict management can guide your efforts.

<br>

We probably shouldn't try to reinvent the wheel.
- Find the good process ideas that are out there and steal from them!

<br>

I liked that Hughes (2007) pushed me to be clearer about problem definitions and to try to see them as separate from strategy.

<br>

I liked that the "policy process model" from Kraft (2011) reminded me that the "right" pressure strategy depends on the stage of the process we are in 
- Arguments that work during agenda setting probably look different from the ones we need during policy formulation

<br>

I liked the C&P (2016) collaborative approach because it makes sense to me that community problem-solving MUST be engaged in serious ways with the people whose behavior we hope to modify.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size55[**Our Problem-Solving Process**]

<br>
.size45[
1. Define the problem (specific and practical)

2. Find the stakeholders

3. Engage with them to refine the problem definition and agree on goals

4. Design a policy...
]

???

At the end of Thursday we started developing our own process for local problem-solving.

### With a couple of days to sleep on it, is there anything you want to change or add here?

#### - Anything from our quick review today we think should be added?

<br>

I've been giving this some thought and I want to throw out a process for how we might proceed.
- I don't want you to assume this is the "right" process.

<br>

Hopefully what was clear to you from our conversation last Thursday is that we will need to be flexible in our approach depending on the specifics of a given problem.

- Some scenarios may call for aggressive action (boycotts) while in others that would be counter-productive

- Some scenarios may require earlier expert or business input due to technical complexity

- etc.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size45[**A Possible (and evolving) Process**]

???

Ok, here we go...

--

<br>

.size40[1) **Argument**: Our community has a problem]

???

### Step 1: Make an explicit argument

This represents your chance to interrogate your own position on a problem
- What are the key assumptions **YOU** are making that set this thing up as a problem
- What data do we have to measure the problem? Don't ask anyone to take your word for it.

--

<br>

.size40[2) **Investigate** the relevant stakeholders]

???

### Step 2: Investigate the players

For each stakeholder:
- How do they define the basic concepts in play?

- What are their preferred sources of wisdom or accountability?

- Fundamentally speaking, do they already see this as a problem?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size45[**A Possible (and evolving) Process**]

<br>

.size40[1) **Argument**: Our community has a problem]

<br>

.size40[2) **Investigate** the relevant stakeholders]

<br>

.size40[3) **Revise** your "we have a problem" argument]

???

### Step 3

You need to adapt your problem framing to draw in as many stakeholders as possible. 

You need each of them to accept that:
1. The problem is real, and
2. This is a collective-choice problem

--

<br>

.size40[4) Consider policy **designs**...]

???

<br>

### Step 4

This is where we'll be headed next in class. 

<br>

### Any questions on this or anything we've reviewed today?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/03-1-stakeholders.webp')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

???

This week we focus on the stakeholders part of our problem-solving process

<br>

Today we'll use the fight over CAFE standards in the US as a means for thinking critically about the role stakeholders play in environmental policy-making.

<br>

We tend to encounter stakeholders through the arguments they make publicly.

However, these statements are strategic signals meant to generate support for their position, not reveal their "true" preferences.

We'll need to do some analytical work in order to use these statements to help us broaden the appeal of our arguments.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/03-1-stakeholders_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center, inverse

.textwhite[.size55[**What can we learn from the stakeholders' public arguments that will help us broaden the appeal of our "we have a problem" argument?**]]

???

The Miller book does a nice job collecting a bunch of public statements by stakeholders on this issue.

Today we'll work to diagram and analyze those arguments.

Remember, our actual key here is to learn more about stakeholders and the arguments they make, not to take a strong position on the CAFE standards.

<br>

### Sound good?

<br>

### Just to set us up on this, what is the problem CAFE standards were designed to solve?

#### - How does Miller define the problem at the start of the chapter?

(SLIDE)


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/03-1-CAFE_Standards_DoE.jpeg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle

???

(Miller argues that no single event triggered this policy)

It was a confluence of many events pointing in the same direction:
- Continued over-dependence on foreign oil from regions in unrest
- Growing concerns over climate change
- Uncertainty about consequences of terrorism
- Domestic manufacturers losing market share to foreign autos with better fuel efficiency

### Per our work over the last few weeks, is this a good definition of the problem? Why or why not?

<br>

Here we see the actual CAFE averages up to 2013 AND the increases mandated by the Obama administration

+ Source is the Dept of Energy

+ You'll note that Obama had planned some pretty sizable increases!

### Did these go into effect? New cars averaging 50+ mpg?

+ (Umm, no. Trump got elected and began rolling back every environmental regulation he could.)

+ (Trump's revised standard: overall avg of 32 miles per gallon in 2026)

SLIDE: So, where are we now?



---

class: middle, slideblue

.size35[
**Biden's New Rule**: Reach a projected industry-wide target of 161 grams CO2 /mile, or 40 miles per gallon value on fuel economy window stickers in 2026.

+ The final emission standards targets for 2023-2026 increase in stringency by between 5-10 percent in each model year.

+ The standards they replace increased in stringency by only 1.5 percent year over year.

+ By MY2026 the final emissions targets are 47 grams of CO2 /mile lower than the standards they replace.
]

???

[LINK to EPA: Final Rule to Revise Existing National GHG Emissions Standards for Passenger Cars and Light Trucks Through Model Year 2026](https://www.epa.gov/regulations-emissions-vehicles-and-engines/final-rule-revise-existing-national-ghg-emissions)


<br>

One of the early climate actions taken by the Biden Administration was to revert the CAFE standards closer to the trajectory put in place by Obama's Administration. 

<br>

Ok, let's dig into the arguments made by the stakeholders in the Miller chapter.

#### Split class into pairs (PLSC with a non-PLSC?)

#### Go sit with your partner!



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, top

.center[.size55[**Analyzing Stakeholders**]]

???

*Assign stakeholders to pairs of students*

1. (Supporter) 3.11 David L. Greene, Oak Ridge National Laboratory (p133-138) 
2. (Opponent) 3.7 The National Center for Public Policy Research (p120-124)
3. (Supporter) 3.9 AND 3.15 The Union of Concerned Scientists (p126-128, 140-141)
4. (Opponent) 3.1 Bill Visnic, SUV Owners of America (p109-112)
5. (Supporter) 3.3 Public Citizen (p113-115)
6. (Opponent) 3.10 The CATO Institute (p128-133)
7. (Supporter) 3.8 Norman Mineta, Secretary of Transportation (p124-126)
8. (Opponent) 3.4 National Review (p115-117)

<br>

#### Pairs, you will now work together to analyze your stakeholder using two prompts I will give you. When you're done, you'll walk us through your findings.

--

<br>

.size45[**1) Diagram the argument**]

???

#### First step: Map out the argument (5 mins)
- Identify the main conclusion, and then the key premises provided to support that conclusion

--

<br>

.size45[**2) Analyze the argument**]
.size35[
- How do they define the problem? 
- What is their preferred source of "wisdom"?
- Where does this argument fit in Munger's Figure 2.4?
- Do they address any counter-arguments?
]

???

#### Second step: Analyze the argument (10 mins)
- You've got the broad strokes of their position mapped out, now we need to go deeper.

### Questions?

Get to it!



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size50[**Analyzing the Stakeholders**]]

.pull-left[
.size35[
- Problem defined?

- Strongest premises?

- Preferred source of "wisdom"?

- Placement in Figure 2.4?

- Counter-arguments?
]]

.pull-right[
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-4.png')
```
]

???

#### YOU: Save time at end for the paper prompt AND plan for Thursday

1. (Supporter) 3.11 David L. Greene, Oak Ridge National Laboratory (p133-138) 
2. (Opponent) 3.7 The National Center for Public Policy Research (p120-124)
3. (Supporter) 3.9 AND 3.15 The Union of Concerned Scientists (p126-128, 140-141)
4. (Opponent) 3.1 Bill Visnic, SUV Owners of America (p109-112)
5. (Supporter) 3.3 Public Citizen (p113-115)
6. (Opponent) 3.10 The CATO Institute (p128-133)
7. (Supporter) 3.8 Norman Mineta, Secretary of Transportation (p124-126)
8. (Opponent) 3.4 National Review (p115-117)

<br>

### Let's hear what you've found!

<br>

### Can we see based on these stakeholder maps enough room for overlap that explains why we got the CAFE standards that we did?

<br>

### Key takeaways for us from today's work? 
#### - Advice for adapting your problem argument to the stakeholders involved?

<br>

Before we wrap today let's talk about the next two weeks.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-green_blue_swirl_side.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle

.center[.size55[**Solving a Local Environmental Problem**]]

<br>

.size40[
**Paper 1: Present the basics of your problem**

Paper 2: Analyze the complicating factors

Paper 3: Propose a policy to address the problem
]

???

Your papers this semester are designed to help you develop some expertise in a local environmental problem and attempt to outline a policy to address it.

Next week you start writing your first paper.

This means you need to get busy selecting your local environmental problem!

SLIDE: the prompt



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-green_blue_swirl_side.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

# Paper 1

.size40[
Make an argument that a specific, local environmental issue is a collective choice problem (e.g. it is a problem for our community and it should be addressed collectively, not privately). 

Be sure to specify the stakeholders in our community most relevant to the problem and the data that supports your argument (don't ask anyone to take your word for it).
]

???

I'll post this description on the Moodle assignment.

<br>

The first paper comes directly from the work we've been doing over the first three weeks of class.

You need to make an argument that the "problem" you selected IS a problem and SHOULD be addressed collectively.

Finding data is key to making your argument convincing that it IS a problem.

Identifying the stakeholders is key to making an argument it is a collective problem.

<br>

### Questions on the prompt?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-green_blue_swirl_side.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle

# Next Tuesday: Brainstorming
.size40[
+ Bring to class a news story or piece of evidence describing a local environmental problem

+ Be ready to present it to the class:
    + What is the specific problem?
    + Who are the key stakeholders?
    + How could we quantify (measure) it?
]

???

To kick off our week of work on the paper NEXT week you have an assignment for Tuesday.

Everybody brings in a case (or two?) we can discuss on Tuesday.

Think of this like a chance to test run some ideas and get feedback from the class.

<br>

### Questions on your job for Tuesday?

<br>

SLIDE: Now as for Thursday this week!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size65[**This Thursday!**]

## Kane Sheek
.size40[
+ Sierra Club - White River Group
+ Ready for 100 Springfield Community Organizer
]

## Todd Wilkinson
.size40[
+ James River Basin Partnership
+ Project Manager
]

???

We have guests coming!

I thought it would be useful and interesting to have some members of the local community who focus on environmental problem-solving.

Our local activists should have lots of information about the landscape of environmental problems in SW MO.

<br>

I'd like this to be a free and easy conversation for them so please come to class ready to engage with them.

Think of this like a chance to examine our assumptions about stakeholders and their roles in the policy-making process!

<br>

SLIDE: I gave each of them a set of questions to kick us off on Thursday.


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size35[
- What is the primary mission of your organization?

- What is your role in the organization?

- What are you working on right now?

- What are the biggest obstacles you face in trying to solve these problems?

- Any advice for successfully solving environmental problems in Springfield / SW MO?

- How can we get involved with your organization?
]

???

I'd like each of you to give this some thought before class.

I wonder if we could steer them towards helping us as we embark on our projects for this semester?

### Any questions?

See you Thursday!
